Field Notes

What large enterprises learn after the AI demo.

The demo is the easy part. The harder question is what happens when AI enters real work: client conversations, advisor preparation, service workflows, CRM, case management, data handoffs, approvals, controls, exceptions, and leadership reporting.

These notes come from that gap — the space between AI capability and operating change.

What I keep seeing

Four truths keep showing up.

Work has to change before AI matters.

Data has to be trusted before AI can act.

Autonomy needs boundaries.

The test is not the demo.

Selected notes

One argument in four moves: work, data, autonomy, systems of action.

Enterprise AI

AI Is Moving Faster Than the Enterprise.

The technology is moving quickly. The harder question is whether the enterprise can change work, governance, data, and decision-making fast enough to use it well.

Data

The Data Problem Hiding Under Every AI Strategy.

Most AI plans talk about models. The harder question is whether trusted context reaches the workflow when a decision has to happen.

Controlled autonomy

Controlled Autonomy Is the Enterprise AI Product.

The useful agent is not the one that does the most. It is the one that acts within the right boundaries and hands the work back at the right moment.

CRM

The CRM Trap: When Systems of Record Never Become Systems of Action.

CRM becomes valuable when it helps the enterprise decide what happens next. Recording activity is not enough.

Together, these notes point to a larger leadership question: can the enterprise redesign work, data, decision-making, and controls fast enough to make AI matter without losing trust?

Discuss the mandate

The useful conversation starts after the demo.

Talk to Chandra about the work, data, controls, and leadership model required to make AI matter inside regulated environments.
